food_subgroup: Seafood noodle dish — rice vermicelli with crustacean

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for lobster white bee hoon. FOODON_03315595 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent class. A more specific term for rice vermicelli seafood dishes does not currently exist in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Crustaceans, cooked) is used as the closest parent. No specific FoodEx2 composite code exists for lobster rice vermicelli soup as a prepared dish. The noodle component is not captured by this code alone.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for lobster white bee hoon as a composite Singaporean dish.

allergens: gluten (rice vermicelli may contain trace gluten depending on brand)

serving.description: Shared dish for 2–3 persons; approximately 350 g per person
serving.natural_language_portion: One realistic serving of lobster white bee hoon for a single diner is approximately 350 g, representing one portion of a shared dish intended for 2–3 people.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from HPB Singapore Foodo — Bee Hoon Soup with Seafood and USDA FoodData Central — Lobster, cooked (SR Legacy #15149)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for lobster white bee hoon as a composite dish. Values are estimated by combining: (1) HPB Foodo data for bee hoon soup with seafood (providing the noodle-broth base macros), and (2) USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked lobster (providing the crustacean protein and fat contribution). The dish is approximately 40% rice vermicelli in broth and 60% lobster meat by edible weight. Evaporated milk adds modest fat and energy. Values represent a weighted composite per 100 g of the ready-to-eat dish. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice vermicelli (boiled)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lobster white bee hoon as a composite dish. GI is estimated from the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source: white rice vermicelli (bee hoon), which has a reported GI of approximately 53 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The lobster and broth components contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrate. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 36.75) / 100 ≈ 19.5, rounded to 19.4. Serving carbohydrate = 10.5 g/100g × 350 g = 36.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from seafood stock and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200 g per sitting, as a 350 g serving provides approximately 1,470 mg sodium — nearly 64% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,300 mg — primarily from the seasoned broth and evaporated milk base.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from white rice vermicelli
type2_diabetes.guidance: The white rice vermicelli base has a GI of approximately 53 (low) but the glycemic load per standard serving (~350 g) is approximately 19–20 g, which is moderate; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol from lobster and saturated fat from evaporated milk and lard
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 350 g serving provides approximately 252 mg dietary cholesterol and 3.85 g saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request preparation without lard and limit frequency of consumption, as the dish is otherwise a good source of lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids from lobster.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lobster White Bee Hoon (龙虾白米粉) is a premium Singaporean Chinese seafood dish featuring whole or halved live lobster cooked in a rich, milky white broth made from evaporated milk, chicken or seafood stock, garlic, ginger, and white pepper, served over or alongside soft-boiled white rice vermicelli (bee hoon). The dish is a signature offering of zi char (煮炒) restaurants in Singapore, most famously associated with establishments such as House of White Bee Hoon. The broth is characteristically creamy, mildly sweet, and umami-rich, with the lobster contributing a sweet, firm, and succulent texture. The rice vermicelli absorbs the broth and becomes silky and flavourful. The dish is typically served as a shared centrepiece for 2–3 diners and commands a premium price point (SGD 50–80 per serving) due to the use of whole lobster. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein dish driven by lobster meat, which is low in fat and rich in vitamin B12, zinc, selenium, and phosphorus. The sodium content is elevated due to the seasoned broth, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic load is moderate owing to the white rice vermicelli base. The dish is not suitable for halal diners unless specifically prepared with halal-certified ingredients, as lard is traditionally used in zi char cooking.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for lobster white bee hoon as a composite dish in HPB Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by combining HPB Foodo bee hoon soup with seafood data and USDA SR Legacy cooked lobster data in a weighted composite. GI is estimated from the analogous rice vermicelli GI value from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because both component sources are approved primary sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; a direct laboratory analysis of this dish as served at Singaporean zi char restaurants would be required for high-confidence values. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in either the HPB Foodo seafood bee hoon entry or the USDA SR Legacy lobster entry. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this composite estimate. 4. Trans fat is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for cooked lobster and is consistent with the absence of partially hydrogenated oils in traditional zi char preparation. 5. The dish is traditionally prepared with lard in zi char restaurants, making it non-halal by default; halal variants exist but are not universal. 6. Serving size of 350 g per person is estimated based on a shared dish of approximately 1,050 g total for 2–3 persons, consistent with typical zi char portion conventions. 7. GI of 53 is assigned based on the analogous rice vermicelli (boiled) value from the University of Sydney GI Database; glycemic_index set to 53 (low) as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source is white rice vermicelli. 8. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy lobster fatty acid profile and are approximate.
